items.correct: Delocalised electrons are free to move through the lattice of positive ions, carrying charge.
items.misconception: Metal atoms themselves move to carry the current.
items.random_wrong: Metals contain naturally moving protons that act as charge carriers.
items.why: Students picture conduction as 'something moving through the wire' and default to whole atoms, since they don't fully grasp delocalisation.

items.q: Why does graphite conduct electricity but diamond does not?
items.correct: Graphite has delocalised electrons between layers; diamond has all four outer electrons in localised covalent bonds.
items.misconception: Graphite conducts because it is a metal, while diamond is a non-metal.
items.random_wrong: Graphite conducts because it can melt easily at room temperature.
items.why: Students associate 'conducts electricity' with 'is a metal' and don't recognise that some non-metallic allotropes have free electrons too.

items.q: What happens to electrons when sodium reacts with chlorine?
items.correct: Sodium loses one electron to chlorine; the resulting Na+ and Cl- ions attract electrostatically.
items.misconception: Sodium and chlorine share an electron between them.
items.random_wrong: Protons move from chlorine to sodium to balance the charges.
items.why: Students over-apply the covalent (sharing) model to all bonding because sharing is introduced first and feels intuitive.

items.q: Solid sodium chloride does NOT conduct electricity because:
items.why: Correct answer: Its ions cannot move. “It has no charged particles” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many delocalised electrons does each carbon atom contribute in graphite?
items.why: The real figure is 1. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
